name: Red Shirt
short_description: Hunt for agates and chalcedony in the rolling badlands of western South Dakota. This remote high plains location offers rockhounders access to classic Great Plains agate deposits on public land.
description:
Red Shirt sits in the heart of the Oglala Lakota County badlands region, where ancient volcanic ash deposits and sedimentary formations have weathered to expose quality agates and jasper specimens. The area's flat to gently rolling terrain at 2,669 feet elevation makes it accessible for collectors of all skill levels, though the remoteness demands preparation. Rockhounders here typically search rocky washes, hillside slopes, and erosion gullies where seasonal water has exposed fresh material. The semi-arid high plains climate keeps vegetation sparse, making visual scanning effective. While the area sees relatively few collectors, the geological potential remains consistent year-round, with better conditions during drier months when recent erosion is visible.

The geology here reflects the High Plains province, where Oligocene and Miocene volcanic activity deposited silica-rich ash and tuff layers. Subsequent weathering and erosion of these formations have freed thousands of agate nodules and jasper fragments into surface contexts. The chalcedony deposits here often display warm earth tones - reds, oranges, and tan hues - typical of iron-oxide influenced formations in this region. Local collectors report occasional finds of banded agate specimens up to 2-3 inches across.

Access is straightforward on public land, though services and facilities are minimal. The flat to gently rolling terrain requires sturdy footwear and sun protection. Water should be brought in, as natural sources are unreliable. The landscape offers wide visibility, reducing navigation concerns despite remoteness. This location rewards patient surface hunting and makes an excellent destination for weekend rockhounding trips into South Dakota's lesser-known collecting areas.
history: The Oglala Lakota County badlands formed through extensive Oligocene and Miocene volcanic activity that deposited thick sequences of ash, tuff, and silica-rich sediments across the High Plains. Subsequent erosion over millions of years has concentrated agate and chalcedony nodules at the surface, creating the collecting opportunities found here today.
tips: Search eroded gullies and wash bottoms where seasonal runoff has exposed fresh agate and chalcedony material - these areas show the most recent finds. Look for the characteristic waxy luster and banded patterns of quality agates; specimens here often display warm orange and red tones from iron oxide staining. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and small shovel to work the softer eroded banks where nodules weather free. Scan the ground systematically from west to east in morning light, which creates better shadows to spot agate's subtle color contrasts against the tan soil.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently rolling high plains terrain at 2,669 feet elevation. Sparse grass and low shrub vegetation typical of semi-arid grassland. Rocky outcrops and eroded washes are primary collecting areas. Footing is relatively stable on dried soil, though early season visits may encounter muddy areas after rain. Expect 0.25 to 1 mile walks from parking areas to productive collecting zones. No significant elevation changes or difficult scrambling required.
best_season: April - June and September - October. Spring and fall offer moderate temperatures ideal for extended field work in this exposed high plains environment. Summer heat exceeds 85 degrees regularly, and winter conditions (November - March) can bring snow and temperature extremes. Late spring rains increase erosion exposure and fresh finds.
written_directions: From Red Shirt, head south on local roads toward the badlands formations. GPS coordinates 43.674132, -102.899086 mark the primary collecting area. Access requires high-clearance vehicle on rough ranch roads; check local conditions before visiting. The site is approximately 2 miles south of Red Shirt townsite. No marked trailhead exists; park safely off active roads and hike into the badlands terrain. Verify current access status with Oglala Lakota County authorities before traveling, as access may be restricted on tribal or private land.
road_conditions: Rough unimproved ranch roads with potential washboard and ruts. High-clearance vehicle required. Road conditions vary seasonally; spring mud and winter snow may close access temporarily. Four-wheel drive recommended during wet conditions.
parking_notes: Informal roadside parking only. Pull completely off active ranch roads. Space limited to a few vehicles. No facilities or marked parking area. Park at least 50 feet from any structures or water sources.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Verify land ownership and obtain permission if on private land. Check Oglala Lakota County regulations before collecting. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment permitted. Respect tribal land boundaries - portions of this region are trust land requiring additional permission.
rules:
Personal use collection only
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Verify land ownership before collecting
Obtain permission for private or tribal land
Respect all posted boundaries and restrictions
nearest_services: Red Shirt (on-site, minimal services). Manderson, South Dakota approximately 15 miles north offers basic supplies. Kyle, South Dakota approximately 20 miles northeast has limited groceries and fuel.
accessibility_notes: Flat to gently rolling terrain accessible to most hikers with basic fitness. Rough ranch road access requires high-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicle. No wheelchair access to productive collecting areas. Sturdy hiking boots recommended for rocky terrain. Exposed landscape offers no shade; sun protection essential.

faq.question: What types of agates and chalcedony can you find at Red Shirt, South Dakota?
faq.answer: Red Shirt is known for banded agates and chalcedony specimens displaying warm orange, red, and tan tones from iron oxide staining. Specimens typically range from 1 to 3 inches across. Jasper and quartz fragments are also common. The Oligocene volcanic deposits here produce classic High Plains agate formations with waxy luster and good color contrast.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Red Shirt free or do you need to pay a fee?
faq.answer: Red Shirt is free public land rockhounding with no permit required. However, you must verify current land ownership before collecting, as portions of this region may be private or tribal trust land requiring permission. Contact Oglala Lakota County authorities to confirm access status before your trip.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Red Shirt, South Dakota?
faq.answer: April through June and September through October are ideal. These seasons offer moderate temperatures perfect for extended collecting in this exposed high plains environment. Spring rains increase erosion and expose fresh material. Summer heat exceeds 85 degrees regularly, while winter brings snow and extreme cold unsuitable for this remote area.

faq.question: How difficult is it to access Red Shirt rockhounding site from nearby towns?
faq.answer: Red Shirt is moderately accessible, located about 15 miles south of Manderson, South Dakota. Rough unimproved ranch roads require high-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicles. Roads are prone to washboard and ruts, and spring mud or winter snow may temporarily close access. The remote high plains location demands preparation and reliable transportation.

faq.question: What tools and preparation do you need for rockhounding at Red Shirt?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots, sun protection, and a hat for the exposed high plains terrain. A small shovel and 5-gallon bucket help work eroded banks where nodules weather free. Bring plenty of water - at least one gallon per person - as natural sources are unreliable in this semi-arid area. A GPS device or map is recommended due to remoteness and lack of marked trails.

faq.question: Is Red Shirt a good rockhounding location for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Red Shirt suits beginners due to its flat terrain and abundant surface material. Agates and chalcedony are relatively easy to spot once you learn their waxy luster and banded patterns. However, the remote location and rough road access require experience with backcountry travel and vehicle preparation. First-time visitors should go with experienced rockhounders familiar with South Dakota badlands conditions.

meta_title: Red Shirt Rockhounding - South Dakota Agate Hunting
meta_description: Find banded agates, chalcedony, and jasper at Red Shirt, South Dakota. Free public land rockhounding in the Oglala Lakota County badlands.
